dimensions.details: All record data is within its TTL validity window
All resolver TTLs are within authoritative limits (RFC 8767 compliant)
12 of 15 expected record types are missing TTL data
All resolvers return consistent data — high source credibility
Some TTLs deviate from typical ranges — may indicate custom configuration

findings.recommendation: NS TTL is below typical — observed 21532s, typical value is 1 day (86400s). Short TTLs increase DNS query volume but enable faster propagation. If you are preparing for a migration or need rapid failover, this may be intentional (RFC 1035 §3.2.1). For steady-state production, consider 86400 seconds per NIST SP 800-53 SI-7 relevance guidance. Use the TTL Tuner for profile-specific recommendations.

SOA TTL is above typical — observed 21532s, typical value is 1 hour (3600s). Long TTLs reduce DNS query volume but slow propagation when records change. Consider 3600 seconds for a balance of performance and flexibility per NIST SP 800-53 SI-7 relevance guidance.

currency_report.guidance: DNS data shows some aging or gaps — consider re-scanning for critical decisions

findings.explanation: SOA Expire is 3 days (259200s). RFC 1912 §2.2 recommends 1,209,600–2,419,200 seconds (14–28 days). If the primary nameserver becomes unreachable, secondary nameservers will stop serving this zone after only 3 days (259200s).

dane_analysis.dnssec_required_note: DANE requires DNSSEC (RFC 6698 §1). TLSA records are only validated when the zone is DNSSEC-signed.
dane_analysis.message: No MX records available — DANE check skipped

Could not retrieve NS TTL from parent zone

dkim_analysis.message: DKIM not discoverable via common selectors (large providers use rotating selectors)

algorithm_observation.label: Adequate
algorithm_observation.quantum_note: All current DNSSEC algorithms use classical cryptography. Post-quantum DNSSEC standards are in active IETF development (draft-sheth-pqc-dnssec-strategy) but no PQC algorithms have been standardized for DNSSEC yet.

dnssec_analysis.message: DNSSEC fully configured and validated — AD (Authenticated Data) flag set by resolver 8.8.8.8 confirming cryptographic chain of trust from root to zone (RFC 4035 §3.2.3)

issues: CDS/CDNSKEY automation present but only single KSK — pre-publish second KSK before rollover
dnssec_ops.message: DNSSEC operational notes: 1 item(s) to review

edge_cdn.message: Domain appears to use direct origin hosting

entries.propagation_note: A records typically propagate within 5 minutes. Some resolvers may cache up to the TTL value.
AAAA records follow the same propagation pattern as A records.
MX record changes may take up to 1 hour to propagate. Mail delivery may be affected during transition.
TXT records (including SPF) typically propagate within 1 hour. Verify with multiple resolvers.
Nameserver changes can take 24–48 hours for full global propagation due to parent zone TTLs.
CNAME changes propagate quickly but downstream records inherit the CNAME TTL.
CAA record changes take effect within TTL. Certificate authorities check at issuance time.
SOA changes propagate to secondaries based on the Refresh interval in the SOA record.
SPF record changes propagate within the TXT record TTL. Test with dig before relying on scan results.
DMARC policy changes at _dmarc subdomain propagate within TTL. Reporting changes take 24–48h to reflect in aggregate reports.
DKIM selector records propagate within TTL. New selectors are available immediately once published; key rotation requires overlap period.
MTA-STS policy changes require updating both the DNS TXT record AND the policy file at /.well-known/mta-sts.txt. The max_age directive in the policy controls how long senders cache it.
TLS-RPT changes propagate within TTL. Report delivery changes take effect in the next reporting period (typically 24 hours).
BIMI record changes propagate within TTL. VMC certificate validation by mail providers may take additional time.
TLSA/DANE records must be published BEFORE rotating TLS certificates. Premature certificate rotation breaks DANE validation.
DNSSEC signing changes (DS record updates at registrar) can take 24–48 hours. Key rollovers require careful timing per RFC 7583.
DANE/TLSA record updates follow the TLSA TTL. Coordinate with TLS certificate lifecycle.

freshness_matrix.guidance: Re-scan times are based on observed TTL values from authoritative and resolver responses. After making DNS changes, wait at least the recommended interval before re-scanning for updated results.

nmap_dns.message: No DNS server misconfigurations found on ns-cloud-d3.googledomains.com

ns_delegation_analysis.enterprise_detail: Nameservers span 2 providers (Google Cloud DNS, Google Domains). Multi-provider DNS provides resilience against single-provider outages — an enterprise best practice for critical domains.
ns_delegation_analysis.note: Parent zone delegation could not be verified

critical_issues: No SPF and no DMARC — domain is completely unprotected against email spoofing. Both protocols are RFC-recommended (not mandatory), but their absence leaves the domain open to impersonation (CVE-2024-7208, CVE-2024-49040)
issues: No SPF record published — RFC 7208 defines the SPF mechanism but does not mandate publication. Without SPF, any server can send email claiming to be this domain (CVE-2024-7208, CVE-2024-7209)

No DMARC record published — RFC 7489 is Informational (not Standards Track); DMARCbis will elevate to Standards Track. Without DMARC, receivers have no policy for handling SPF/DKIM failures — spoofed mail may be delivered (CVE-2024-49040)
posture.label: Critical Risk
posture.message: No SPF or DMARC records — domain is unprotected against email spoofing
monitoring: DKIM signing inferred from provider — could not directly verify selector
recommendations: Publish an SPF record to authorize legitimate mail senders
Publish a DMARC record starting with p=none and rua reporting

all_fixes.dns_purpose: Instructs receiving servers to reject all email from this domain — no legitimate mail is expected.
all_fixes.fix: This domain has no MX records and appears to be a website-only domain. A DMARC reject policy tells receiving mail servers to reject any email claiming to be from your domain.
all_fixes.rfc_title: Domain-based Message Authentication, Reporting, and Conformance (DMARC)
all_fixes.title: Add DMARC Reject for No-Mail Domain

all_fixes.dns_purpose: Explicitly declares no servers are authorized to send email from this domain.
all_fixes.fix: This domain has no MX records and appears to be a website-only domain. Publishing a strict SPF record explicitly declares that no servers are authorized to send email, preventing attackers from spoofing your domain.
all_fixes.rfc_title: Sender Policy Framework (SPF) for Authorizing Use of Domains in Email, Version 1
all_fixes.title: Lock Down SPF for No-Mail Domain

all_fixes.dns_host_help: (root of domain — adjust CA to match your provider)
all_fixes.dns_purpose: CAA constrains which CAs can issue certificates for this domain.
all_fixes.fix: CAA records specify which Certificate Authorities may issue certificates for your domain, reducing the risk of unauthorized certificate issuance.

all_fixes.title: Add CAA Records
